column2: In RPC, what is the role of the client-side *stub*?
column3: It is the actual function running on the server that the client wants to invoke.
column4: It pretends to be the remote function, hiding all network communication from the caller.
column5: It caches results of previous remote calls to avoid unnecessary round-trips.
column6: It provides authentication credentials for every remote call.

column2: RPC uses copy-and-restore semantics for reference parameters. Is this equivalent to call-by-reference?
column3: Yes — the result is always identical to call-by-reference.
column4: No — they differ when the same variable is passed for two parameters (aliasing), as shown by f(&a, &a).
column5: Yes, as long as the callee does not modify the parameter.
column6: No — copy-and-restore always produces a wrong result because the copy is delayed.

column2: A local procedure call executes exactly once. Why can RPC not guarantee exactly-once semantics?
column3: RPC is too slow to detect whether the call was already executed.
column4: Failures such as lost messages or server crashes mean the call may not have executed or may have executed multiple times, with no way to distinguish these cases reliably.
column5: Exactly-once is achievable with a sufficiently large sequence-number space.
column6: IDL limitations prevent the stub from tracking invocations.

column2: A lost RPC reply looks identical to a lost request, so the client retransmits. When can the server simply recompute and resend the result without keeping a reply cache?
column3: Always — recomputing is always safe and correct.
column4: Only when the operation is idempotent: applying it twice leaves the system in the same state as applying it once.
column5: Only when the client includes a checksum in the retransmitted request.
column6: Never — a reply cache is always required for correct RPC behaviour.

column2: Which architectural property is the KEY distinguishing constraint of REST compared to generic web services?
column3: REST always uses JSON; SOAP-based web services always use XML.
column4: REST mandates a uniform interface: resources are identified by URIs, manipulated via a fixed set of HTTP methods, and representations contain links to discover further resources (HATEOAS).
column5: REST is stateful; SOAP-based web services are stateless.
column6: REST requires WSDL for interface description; generic web services do not.

column2: Which URL and method pattern is most RESTful for a book collection API?
column4: GET /books/ to list, POST /books/ to create, DELETE /books/42/ to remove one

column2: In a microservices architecture, what is the key difference between orchestration and choreography?
column3: Orchestration uses HTTP; choreography uses message queues exclusively.
column4: In orchestration a central service controls the flow and dependencies; in choreography each service reacts to events independently with no central controller.
column5: Orchestration always scales better; choreography does not scale.
column6: Choreography requires a shared central database; orchestration does not.

column2: An image-processing microservice is overwhelmed with requests. You choose to scale out rather than scale up. What does this mean and what challenge does it introduce?
column3: Scale out means moving to a faster machine; the challenge is cost.
column4: Scale out means running multiple instances of the service; the challenge is discovering instances and distributing work across them (service discovery and load balancing).
column5: Scale out means reducing the service's feature set to lower CPU usage.
column6: Scale out means moving the service to the cloud; the challenge is increased network latency.

column2: gRPC defines services and messages in .proto files. What does the protoc compiler generate from these files?
column4: Client stubs and server skeletons in the target programming language, along with serialisation and deserialisation code.
column5: HTML documentation pages for the API.
column6: A WSDL file compatible with SOAP-based web services.

column2: Ten microservices all share one central SQL database. What architectural problem does this cause, and what is the recommended remedy?
column3: No problem — a central database is the standard and correct pattern for microservices.
column4: The database becomes a bottleneck and a tight coupling point; each service should own its own database and services should communicate via events or message queues.
column5: The problem is authentication; a service registry solves it.
column6: The problem is schema incompatibility; replace the SQL database with a single NoSQL store.
